turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-909, Kousisis v. United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: This case presents the question whether a defendant may be convicted of wire fraud even if he did not intend to cause the victim net financial loss. Here are the facts. Mr. Kousisis, the defendant below, was a project manager for Alpha Painting and Construction Company. When Kousisis learned that the Pennsylvania Department of Transportation,I'll call that PennDOT, was soliciting bids for two painting projects in Philadelphia, he submitted a bid for each project on Alpha's behalf, and Alpha won the contracts. Because the federal government was partially funding the projects, PennDOT, and in turn, Alpha and Kousisis, had to play by the federal government's rules, including its rules governing disadvantaged business participation. In short, federal grant recipients, like PennDOT, must ensure that socially and economically disadvantaged small businesses participate in the projects that the federal government funds. Consistent with this requirement, Alpha and Kousisis promised that they would obtain their painting materials from a disadvantaged supplier. They lied. As the government later learned, Alpha and Kousisis devised a scheme to obtain materials from a less expensive supplier that was not disadvantaged, and because they used fraudulent purchase orders and false compliance certifications to cover their tracks, PennDOT was none the wiser. The painting projects progressed and were eventually completed, and by the time the last coat of paint was dry, Alpha had turned a gross profit of over $20 million. PennDOT, for its part, was satisfied with Alpha's work. But then, the government discovered Alpha and Kousisis' disadvantaged business deception. Alpha and Kousisis were indicted for wire fraud, and after the jury rendered a guilty verdict, they moved for an acquittal. Emphasizing that Alpha's paintwork had met expectations, they argued that PennDOT had received the full economic benefit of its bargain. Sure, Alpha might not have obtained its supplies from a disadvantaged business, but their scheme had not left PennDOT financially worse off. No matter, the government responded. The theory under which Alpha and Kousisis had been tried and convicted what the parties call the fraudulent-inducement theory doesn't require the government to prove that the defendant sought to leave the victim economically worse off. Under this theory, a defendant commits fraud whenever he induces a victim into a contract or other transaction under materially false pretenses. And that's exactly what Alpha and Kousisis did here, the government argued. After all, they had lied about their plans for obtaining Alpha's paint supplies. And had PennDOT known the truth, it wouldn't have awarded Alpha the painting contracts. If it had, it likely would have violated federal law. The District Court and the Third Circuit agreed with the government. Both courts held that the fraudulent-inducement theory is a legitimate basis for securing a wire fraud conviction. As the Third Circuit put it, Alpha and Kousisis had set out to obtain millions of dollars that they would not have received but for their fraudulent misrepresentations. We agree with the courts below. The fraudulent-inducement theory is consistent with the text of the wire fraud statute, the common law, and our precedent. So we reject petitioners' argument that a fraud conviction requires the government to prove that the defendant aimed to inflict economic loss. Start with the statute. It prohibits devising or intending to devise a scheme to obtain money or property by means of false or fraudulent pretenses. Alpha and Kousisis' scheme plainly satisfied each of these elements. They devised a scheme to induce PennDOT to award them painting contracts and, in turn, to obtain PennDOT's money by falsely representing their plans for acquiring Alpha's paint supplies. And while it's true that Alpha and Kousisis provided something in return, painting, high-quality painting,that doesn't matter. After all, the statute uses the phrase "money or property", and money or property is no less obtained simply because something else is given in return. Petitioners next urge us to look to the common law of fraud. Common law courts, they say, have long required fraud victims to prove that they sustained economic loss. Not so. At common law, "fraud" was a word of many meanings, and while some courts required economic loss in some circumstances, the general rule was that a fraud was complete when the defendant induced the victim to hand over her money or property under materially false pretenses. In other words, no economic loss required. And that's enough to render the common law unhelpful to Alpha and Kousisis. Finally, petitioners say that the fraudulent-inducement theory is inconsistent with our precedent. Again, they're wrong. In fact, we have twice rejected the argument that a fraud conviction depends on economic loss. And while our precedent sets forth a number of rules about the interests that federal fraud statutes protect, the fraudulent-inducement theory is in harmony with those rules. It is not, as petitioners argue, in tension with them. Nearly a century ago, the esteemed Judge Learned Hand wrote that "a man is nonetheless cheated out of his property when he is induced to part with it by fraud, even though he gets a quid pro quo of equal value." We agree. Because the Third Circuit's judgment comports with the federal wire fraud statute and our precedent interpreting it, we affirm. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Gorsuch has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment. And Justice Sotomayor has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment.
